learning_outcomes.description: Explain the law and rules of conduct and regulatory processes applicable to the legal profession (with particular reference to Queensland) and its rules of conduct.
Explain the legal requirements applicable to lawyers for dealing with trust money.
Apply knowledge of legal ethics to the practise of law, drawing on prominent philosophies and the moral and societal importance for ethical conduct by lawyers.
Reflect on your personal ethical framework, how it relates to and impacts on important aspects of legal practice.
Apply advanced decision-making skills to ethical issues arising in legal practice.

assessments.task_description:
This assessment assesses your ability to independently research a topic related to legal professional ethics, and to demonstrate your ability to apply this research, as well as your learning from the course, in an in depth and critical way within an essay format. 

This essay is therefore testing your ability to identify useful knowledge, explain it accurately and then apply these learnings to help you think about a topic in a critical but accurate and balanced way, and then provide your own conclusions about it.

You should apply your learning from the course, as well as your own views and the thoughts of others in scholarly writing and/or case law. You must:

1. make some reference to, and reflect upon, the relevant law and professional norm associated with the topic; AND

2. provide a clear identification of the relevant ethical issues; AND

3. demonstrate appropriate research undertaken for the subject of your essay.

You should select one of the topics set for the essay. The essay should reflect your independent and original take on a specified topic, clearly express your own views and apply scholarship/law/knowledge from your research on the topic. It should be more than a description of a particular debate or issue and while describing what others have said in the area is important, you need to build on this with your own thoughts and connections between ideas. Be critical, but accurate, in how you consider the research material found. You must put forward points that support and oppose your views, even where you might provide more words and weight to your own preference. Explain why the other side is not convincing.

The total word count for the research essay must not be more than 1,500 words (excluding footnotes (which must not contain substantive material)). Words in excess of the limit will not be taken into consideration in awarding a mark. Please state the total word count for your essay on the front page (excluding footnotes). Please provide a bibliography which is not included in the word count. There is no minimum word length. However, you should be aware of the possibility that a significantly under-length piece submitted may indicate that the topic has not been covered in sufficient depth or with appropriate sophistication.

You are welcome to discuss your topic with anyone during or after the course. However, the essay is an individual piece of work. It must be entirely your own work in composition, writing and research undertaken. Any use of AI must be explained (see Law School declaration sheet) and the essay must be your work not taken from/written by AI. As usual, anyone else's work referred to must be properly cited. As for any essay, the marker will expect appropriate referencing of sources and communication of information and ideas according to UQ conventions for legal essay writing. Please refer to the rubric on Blackboard.

There is no minimum or maximum number of cited articles/cases to satisfy the research element. However, to provide some general guidance, it is expected that more than three articles/secondary literature are used in your discussion. No marks are awarded for the number of articles referred to alone. Marks will be allocated for the use of the material found to add to your argument.

If you have a query or concern about the content of your essay, you should speak to the course coordinator well before the due date for the essay.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit your answer in Word format to Blackboard essay submission folder by the deadline. All answers are required to be submitted using Turnitin. You can submit a draft of your assignment to Turnitin prior to submission of the final assignment and receive a report.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
This task requires students to reflect on their views or experiences in relation to a professional ethical topic. Students must refer to an ethical theory (or theories) discussed in the course, including at least one set text, as well as their own values. Students will learn about the process of reflection and reflective professionalism during the course and will be expected to draw on this learning in completing this task. Essays that simply describe the situation or the law or secondary article considered will perform poorly.

You are encouraged to discuss your topic with anyone during or after the course. However, the reflection is an individual piece of work. It must be entirely your own work in composition and writing. Any use of AI must be explained (see Law School declaration sheet) and the reflection must be your work not taken from/written by AI. As usual, anyone else's work referred to must be properly cited. The marker will expect appropriate referencing of sources and communication of information and ideas according to UQ conventions for legal essay writing. Refer to the marking rubric provided on Blackboard.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Please submit a document in Word through Blackboard link provided.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Students must undertake the end-of-semester exam worth 50% of the marks for the course. The in person exam will be administered by central Examinations. Further details of the assessment dates and delivery will be provided during semester as advised by the University.

Examinable topics

All aspects of the course are potentially examinable in the final exam except:

- general historical and demographic background discussed in Week 1 materials; and

- legal ethics theories and decision making approaches discussed in the course.

The material delivered by guest lecturers are examinable. All of the examinable topics are covered in the course weekly readings. However, there is also an expectation that students read and refer to prescribed cases, as well as power point slides, as well.

Format and allowable materials in the exam

Any written or printed material is permitted in the exam; it may also be annotated. Answers should refer to and cite relevant case law and legislation/rules but should not include footnotes or a bibliography. There will be two questions of different weighting.

Students must answer two questions. There will be a total of 50 marks available.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 1 To Week 13(27 Jul - 01 Nov)
learning_activities.topic:
Lectures

  • Who, what why lawyers? The first (online recorded) lecture will introduce the course aims and content and provide context.
  • 1. Admission to law and professional discipline
  • 2. Establishing the lawyer-client relationship and billing ethically
  • 3. Confidentiality and legal professional privilege
  • 4. Competent lawyering
  • 5. Advocates' ethical roles and duties - part 1
  • 6. Advocacy ethical roles and duties - part 2
  • 7. Duties to the client and the court and ethical theories
  • 8. Conflicting duties/loyalties- part 1
  • 9. Conflicting duties/loyalties - part 2
  • 10. Trust money and dealing with others' money ethically
  • 11. Lawyers' duties to third parties and to all
  • 12. Ending the lawyer-client relationship
  • 13. Being ethical and doing justice, and revising the course

Learning outcomes: L01, L02

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 1 To Week 13(27 Jul - 01 Nov)
learning_activities.topic:
Workshops

  • 1. What sort of lawyer do you want to be?
  • 2. Being a 'fit and proper person'
  • 3. How to bill clients for work ethically
  • 4. Keeping secrets: confidentiality and legal professional privilege
  • 5. A client's best interests
  • 6. To be or not to be a zealous advocate?

No week 7 workshop

  • 8. Legal ethics and reflective professionalism
  • 9. Identifying and balancing competing interests

No week 10 workshop.

  • 11. Conflicts - manage or avoid?
  • 12. Should we care about anyone other than the client?
  • 13. Revision session for end-of-semester exam

Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04, L05
